ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for 'hot and spicy boiled pork slices rice set' as a composite dish. Mapped to broader class 'pork dish' (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible anchor. Rice component not separately coded.

serving.description: 1 set meal (pork slices + rice + drink + pickled radish)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal portion comprising approximately 150–180 g of hot and spicy pork slices served over 200 g of steamed white rice, accompanied by a choice of drink and a small serving of pickled radish, totalling approximately 500 g.

nutrient_source.title: Hot & Spicy Pork Slices Rice Set — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Hot & Spicy Pork Slices Rice Set' (cr_id G00010500). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB entry and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. GI estimated at 68 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) blended with the pork and vegetable components which lower the composite meal GI. The composite meal GI of approximately 68 is consistent with published mixed rice-and-protein meal GI values in Atkinson et al. (2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (68) × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100. Serving carbohydrate estimated at 126.5 g (25.19 g/100g × 500 g serving). GL = 68 × 126.5 / 100 = 86.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; set meal at 500 g delivers approximately 1155 mg sodium, representing 50–58% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and chili oil seasoning, avoid consuming the full drink portion if it is sweetened or sodium-containing, and note that the potassium content (327 mg/100 g; ~1635 mg per set) partially offsets sodium load in line with DASH-diet principles, but total meal sodium remains a meaningful contribution to daily intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load from white rice (25.19 g/100 g; ~126 g per set) with medium composite GI of approximately 68; dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g partially moderates postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller rice portion or substitute brown rice to reduce glycaemic load, note that the 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre and protein (6.5 g/100 g) slow glucose absorption, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the medium GI and high total carbohydrate content of the full set meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork (red meat) contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid; chili oil adds calorie-dense fat; sodium at 231 mg/100 g elevates blood pressure risk; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100 g in HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, request minimal chili oil and soy sauce, consider substituting pork with a leaner protein such as chicken breast or tofu, and note that the iron (2.55 mg/100 g) and potassium (327 mg/100 g) content are beneficial micronutrients within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies RA inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6; chili is a nightshade ingredient that may trigger joint inflammation in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the pro-inflammatory profile of pork (red meat, arachidonic acid) and chili (nightshade); substituting pork with chicken breast or tofu and omitting chili oil would substantially reduce inflammatory risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot and Spicy Pork Slices Rice Set (水煮肉片饭套餐, Shuǐ Zhǔ Ròu Piàn Fàn Tào Cān) is a Chinese-Singaporean set meal featuring thinly sliced pork poached in a fiery Sichuan-style broth of doubanjiang (spicy fermented bean paste), dried chili, Sichuan peppercorn, garlic, ginger, and soy sauce, then finished with a drizzle of hot chili oil. The dish is served over a generous portion of steamed white rice, accompanied by a choice of drink and a side of crunchy pickled radish. The pork slices are tender and silky from the poaching method, while the sauce is intensely spicy, numbing (from Sichuan peppercorn), and savoury. Per 100 g, the set delivers 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, 231 mg sodium, 327 mg potassium, 54 mg calcium, 40 mg magnesium, and 2.55 mg iron. The composite meal GI is estimated at approximately 68 (medium), driven primarily by the white rice component. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension (sodium ~1155 mg per set), moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes (high carbohydrate load from rice, medium GI), and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease (red meat, chili oil fat, sodium). It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the pro-inflammatory profile of pork and chili. The dish is popular at Chinese food courts and hawker centres in Singapore as a lunch or dinner option.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010500, confidence 0.95 in source). HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. All core macros are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from analogous approved sources with documented basis.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite set meal is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010500) as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact match for this composite Singaporean set meal. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB source entry for this dish. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g in the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB canonical record and is plausible for a composite rice-dominant dish where the pork cholesterol is diluted across the full set weight. 4. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on the dominant white rice component (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted downward for the protein and fibre contributions of the pork and vegetable components; this is consistent with published mixed rice-and-protein meal GI values. 5. Serving size of 500 g is an estimate for a standard set meal; actual vendor portion may vary. 6. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are broader-category matches; no exact ontology code exists for this composite dish.
